reasoning:
The latest known value for WLCFLL up to the forecast date of June 8 is 4668 (for the week ending June 4). With the BTFP wind-down complete, the series has settled into a baseline of pure discount window usage (primary/secondary/seasonal credit), which has averaged around 4,300 over the last 15 weeks with random fluctuations. Because 4,668 is elevated relative to this recent average (roughly the 70th-75th percentile of post-March data), short-term mean reversion suggests a strong likelihood of a decrease in the next few weeks. Therefore, the probability of the June 15 reading exceeding 4668 is low (~31%). 

As the forecasting horizon extends to July, September, and December, the strict short-term mean-reversion assumption weakens. Given that discount window borrowing is right-skewed—possessing a firm lower bound but no upper bound during periods of banking stress or year-end liquidity crunches—the probability of exceeding the threshold naturally drifts upward over longer horizons due to increased uncertainty and the potential for outlier spikes. Consequently, my probabilities smooth toward the 0.50 anchor as time increases.
